en.extract:
The black-headed tody-flycatcher (Todirostrum nigriceps) is a species of bird in the family Tyrannidae.
It is found in Colombia, Costa Rica, Ecuador, Panama, and Venezuela.
Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests. It is a small, black and yellow, insectivorous bird. 
Its call and song are composed of high pitched one-note chirps. Each vocalization is often confounded for the other. Its reproductive traits are not well documented but both parents contribute to building the nest and clutches only have one egg at a time. In 2021, the IUCN assessed its conservation status and deemed it of least concern. 


Description
The black-headed tody flycatcher is a small bird with a glossy black head and a white throat. The black coloring continues down the sides of the neck. It has bright yellow underparts and olive-yellow upperparts. The wings are black with two yellow wingbars and yellow streaking and the tail is short and black. Its head is relatively large, as is its straight black bill. It has dark irises and no eye-rings. Its legs are blueish-gray.  
The females and males have the same coloring. Adults are between 8–10.2 cm long and weigh 6–6.5 g. Juveniles have slightly duller plumage, pale yellow underparts and a vaguely yellow throat. Their heads are also duller black, and their wingbars are more olive-tinged.
The black-headed tody flycatcher is often confused with the common flycatcher (Todirostrum cinereum), which has a yellow throat and white eye-ring. Other similar species include the painted tody-flycatcher (Todirostrum pictum) which has black-streaked underparts and a yellow throat, and the yellow-browed tody-flycatcher (Todirostrum chrysocrotaphum) which has a yellow and black throat and yellow supercilium.


Taxonomy
The black-headed tody-flycatcher is a member of the tyrant flycatcher family Tyrannidae. The genus Todirostrum was first described by French naturalist René Lesson in 1831. The name combines the genus Todus and the Latin "rostrum" which means bill. Within the Todirostrum genus, there are seven species. The species name "nigriceps" is Latin for "black-headed," hence its common name. The species was first described by Philip Sclater in 1855. Its holotype is in the British Museum of Natural History, in London.
Currently, no subspecies is recognized for the black-headed tody-flycatcher. The genus Poecilotriccus makes a monophyletic clade with Todirostrum that is considered basal to the other genera in the Tyrannidae family.


Habitat and distribution
The black-headed tody-flycatcher is a non-migratory neotropical species found mainly in southern Central America and north-western South America. It is present in eastern Costa Rica, Panama, and north and western Colombia. It has also been recorded in far west Venezuela and western Ecuador. Although it has been found at altitudes of 1500 m, this species does not extend its range into the Andes. Rather, it borders the mountains and is occasionally found in the valleys between the Colombian cordilleras.
This flycatcher is a tree-dwelling species, that sticks to the canopy and edges of humid forests. It is highly selective in its habitat, choosing mostly the tallest trees. It has also been found in second-growth forests, where human disturbance has impacted the growth and species present. 


Behavior


Vocalizations
This species' call is a single high-pitched "peep". Its song is a series of 5 to 8 high-pitched "jyip" notes that are quickly repeated. They start evenly spaced and accelerate slightly at the end, increasing in pitch slightly. It often calls repeatedly, which can be easily confused with their song. The chirps also sound insect-like. 


Diet
The black-headed tody-flycatcher is insectivorous. It targets flowering trees to find its meals, looking for insects that flowers attract. Its main diet includes various arthropods: such as beetles, flies, spiders, damselflies, ants, and parasitoid wasps. It forages alone, or sometimes in pairs, inside dense foliage and along canopy edges. 
To ensure access to food, tody-flycatchers have evolved diets consisting of mostly non-overlapping arthropod species. Each species focuses on a select group of insects to feed on and avoids insect species eaten by other tody-flycatchers.


Reproduction
Black-headed tody flycatchers build nests using plant fibers and line them with down feathers. They often build their nests near wasp nests, assuring a nearby food source. Both adults in the parental unit contribute to building the nest. They thread plant material together into nests that hang from tree branches. Nesting times vary by location, but nesting tends to occur between June and August and adults have been found in breeding conditions in February. Clutches tend to be of a singular egg. The egg is white with sparse yellow-brown spotting and rust ends.
Occasionally, Piratic flycatchers (Legatus leucophaius) have usurped the black-headed tody-flycatchers' nests to use as their own.  


Conservation
The IUCN most recently assessed it in 2021 and classified it as "least concern". Though its population is declining, its wide distribution and high estimated population numbers do not qualify it for the "near-threatened" status.


References


External links
Black-headed tody-flycatcher, Birds of the World
IUCN Red List - Assessment of Black-headed tody-flycatcher


Photographs
Black-headed tody-flycatcher - PSeubert, Flickr (side profile)
Black-headed Tody-Flycatcher - Roger Wasley, Flickr (close-up)


Vocalizations
Listen to the call
Listen to the song

The '''black-headed tody-flycatcher''' ('''''Todirostrum nigriceps''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Tyrannidae]].
It is found in [[Colombia]], [[Costa Rica]], [[Ecuador]], [[Panama]], and [[Venezuela]].
Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s. It is a small, black and yellow, [[Insectivore|insectivorous]] bird. 

Its call and song are composed of high pitched one-note [[Chirp|chirps]]. Each [[Bird vocalization|vocalization]] is often confounded for the other. Its reproductive traits are not well documented but both parents contribute to building the [[nest]] and [[Clutch (eggs)|clutches]] only have one [[egg]] at a time. In 2021, the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]] assessed its [[conservation status]] and deemed it of [[Least-concern species|least concern]]. 

== Description ==
The black-headed tody flycatcher is a small bird with a glossy black head and a white throat. The black coloring continues down the sides of the neck. It has bright yellow underparts and olive-yellow upperparts. The wings are black with two yellow wingbars and yellow streaking and the tail is short and black. Its head is relatively large, as is its straight black [[Beak|bill]]. It has dark [[Iris (anatomy)|irises]] and no [[Eye-ring|eye-rings]]. Its legs are blueish-gray.  

The females and males have the same coloring. Adults are between 8–10.2 cm long and weigh 6–6.5 g. Juveniles have slightly duller [[plumage]], pale yellow underparts and a vaguely yellow throat. Their heads are also duller black, and their wingbars are more olive-tinged.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Walther |first=Bruno |date=2020 |title=Black-headed Tody-Flycatcher (Todirostrum nigriceps), version 1.0 |journal=Birds of the World |doi=10.2173/bow.bhtfly1.01}}</ref>

The black-headed tody flycatcher is often confused with the [[Common tody-flycatcher|common flycatcher ''(Todirostrum cinereum)'']]'','' which has a yellow throat and white eye-ring.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Walther |first=Bruno |date=2020 |title=Common Tody-Flycatcher (Todirostrum cinereum), version 1.0 |journal=Birds of the World |doi=10.2173/bow.cotfly1.01}}</ref> Other similar species include the [[Painted tody-flycatcher|painted tody-flycatcher (''Todirostrum pictum'')]] which has black-streaked underparts and a yellow throat, and the [[Yellow-browed tody-flycatcher|yellow-browed tody-flycatcher (''Todirostrum chrysocrotaphum'')]] which has a yellow and black throat and yellow [[supercilium]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Walther |first=Bruno |date=2020 |title=Painted Tody-Flycatcher (Todirostrum pictum), version 1.0 |journal=Birds of the World |doi=10.2173/bow.patfly1.01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Schulenberg |first1=Thomas S. |last2=Kirwan |first2=Guy M. |date=2020 |title=Yellow-browed Tody-Flycatcher (Todirostrum chrysocrotaphum), version 1.0 |journal=Birds of the World |doi=10.2173/bow.ybtfly1.01}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
The black-headed tody-flycatcher is a member of the [[tyrant flycatcher]] family Tyrannidae.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last1=del Hoyo |first1=J. |title=HBW and BirdLife International Illustrated Checklist of the Birds of the World. Volume 2: Passerines. |last2=Collar |first2=N.J. |last3=Christie |first3=D.A. |last4=Elliott |first4=A |last5=Fishpool |first5=L.D.C. |last6=Boesman, P. |first6=P. |last7=Kirwan |first7=G.M. |date=2016 |publisher=Lynx Edicions and BirdLife International, Barcelona, Spain and Cambridge, UK. |pages=374}}</ref> The [[Todirostrum|genus ''Todirostrum'']] was first described by French [[Natural history|naturalist]] [[René Lesson]] in 1831.<ref>{{Cite book |title=Priority! the dating of scientific names in ornithology: a directory to the literature and its reviewers |date=2011 |publisher=Aves Press |isbn=978-0-9568611-1-5 |editor-last=Dickinson |editor-first=Edward C. |location=Northampton |editor-last2=Overstreet |editor-first2=Leslie K. |editor-last3=Dowsett |editor-first3=Robert J. |editor-last4=Bruce |editor-first4=Murray D.}}</ref> The name combines the [[Todus|genus ''Todus'']] and the [[Latin]] "[[Rostrum (anatomy)|rostrum]]" which means bill.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Jobling |first=James A. |title=The Helm dictionary of scientific bird names: from aalge to zusii |date=2010 |publisher=Christopher Helm |isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 |location=London}}</ref> Within the ''Todirostrum'' genus, there are seven species.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Tyrant flycatchers – IOC World Bird List |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/flycatchers/ |access-date=2024-10-10 |website=www.worldbirdnames.org}}</ref> The species name "''nigriceps"'' is Latin for "black-headed," hence its common name.<ref name=":0" /> The species was first described by [[Philip Sclater]] in 1855. Its [[holotype]] is in the [[Natural History Museum, London|British Museum of Natural History]], in [[London]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Catalog Record: Catalogue of the birds in the British Museum {{!}} HathiTrust Digital Library |url=https://catalog.hathitrust.org/api/volumes/oclc/4686548.html |access-date=2024-10-11 |website=catalog.hathitrust.org}}</ref>

Currently, no [[subspecies]] is recognized for the black-headed tody-flycatcher.<ref name=iucn/> The [[Poecilotriccus|genus ''Poecilotriccus'']] makes a [[Monophyly|monophyletic]] [[clade]] with ''Todirostrum'' that is considered [[Basal (phylogenetics)|basal]] to the other genera in the Tyrannidae family.<ref>{{cite thesis |last1=Tello |first1=Jose G |title=Understanding neotropical diversification: Implications from phylogenetic patterns in two avian genera |date=2004 |id={{ProQuest|305066745}}}}</ref>

== Habitat and distribution ==
The black-headed tody-flycatcher is a non-[[Bird migration|migratory]] [[Neotropical realm|neotropical]] species found mainly in southern [[Central America]] and north-western [[South America]]. It is present in eastern Costa Rica, Panama, and north and western Colombia. It has also been recorded in far west Venezuela and western Ecuador.<ref name=iucn/> Although it has been found at [[Altitude|altitudes]] of 1500 m, this species does not extend its range into [[Andes|the Andes]]. Rather, it borders the [[Mountain|mountains]] and is occasionally found in the [[Valley|valleys]] between the Colombian [[Cordillera|cordilleras]].<ref name=":1" />

This flycatcher is a [[tree-dwelling]] species, that sticks to the [[Canopy (biology)|canopy]] and edges of humid forests. It is highly selective in its habitat, choosing mostly the tallest trees. It has also been found in [[Secondary forest|second-growth forests]], where human disturbance has impacted the growth and species present.<ref name=":1" /> 

== Behavior ==

=== Vocalizations ===
This species' call is a single high-pitched "peep". Its [[Bird vocalization|song]] is a series of 5 to 8 high-pitched "jyip" notes that are quickly repeated. They start evenly spaced and accelerate slightly at the end, increasing in pitch slightly.<ref name=":1" /> It often calls repeatedly, which can be easily confused with their song. The [[Chirp|chirps]] also sound [[insect]]-like. 

=== Diet ===
The black-headed tody-flycatcher is insectivorous. It targets [[Flower|flowering]] trees to find its meals, looking for insects that flowers attract.<ref name=":3">{{Cite book |last1=Stiles |first1=F.G. |title=A guide to the birds of Costa Rica. |last2=Skutch |first2=A.F. |last3=Gardner |first3=D. |date=1989 |publisher=Cornell University Press. |isbn=9780801496004 |location=Ithica |pages=330}}</ref> Its main diet includes various [[Arthropod|arthropods]]: such as [[Beetle|beetles]], [[Fly|flies]], [[Spider|spiders]], [[Damselfly|damselflies]], [[Ant|ants]], and [[Parasitoid wasp|parasitoid wasps]].<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sherry |first1=Thomas W. |title=Comparative Dietary Ecology of Sympatric, Insectivorous Neotropical Flycatchers (Tyrannidae) |journal=Ecological Monographs |date=September 1984 |volume=54 |issue=3 |pages=313–338 |doi=10.2307/1942500 |jstor=1942500 }}</ref> It [[Foraging|forages]] alone, or sometimes in pairs, inside dense [[foliage]] and along canopy edges.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Willis |first1=Edwin O’Neill |last2=Bencke |first2=Glayson Ariel |title=Southeastern Brazilian tyrannulets as flower watchers |journal=Revista Brasileira de Ornitologia |date=September 2019 |volume=27 |issue=3 |pages=158–163 |doi=10.1007/BF03544465 |doi-access=free }}</ref> 

To ensure access to food, tody-flycatchers have evolved diets consisting of mostly non-overlapping arthropod species. Each species focuses on a select group of insects to feed on and avoids insect species eaten by other tody-flycatchers.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sherry |first1=Thomas W |last2=Kent |first2=Cody M |last3=Sánchez |first3=Natalie V |last4=Şekercioğlu |first4=Çağan H |title=Insectivorous birds in the Neotropics: Ecological radiations, specialization, and coexistence in species-rich communities |journal=The Auk |date=24 December 2020 |volume=137 |issue=4 |doi=10.1093/auk/ukaa049 }}</ref>

=== Reproduction ===
Black-headed tody flycatchers build [[Nest|nests]] using plant [[Fiber|fibers]] and line them with [[Down feather|down feathers]]. They often build their nests near wasp nests, assuring a nearby food source.<ref name=":1" /> Both adults in the parental unit contribute to building the nest.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ferreira |first1=Dalila de Fátima |last2=Aquino |first2=Marla Mendes de |last3=Heming |first3=Neander Marcel |last4=Marini |first4=Miguel Ângelo |last5=Leite |first5=Felipe Sá Fortes |last6=Lopes |first6=Leonardo Esteves |title=Breeding in the gray-headed tody-flycatcher (Aves: Tyrannidae) with comments on geographical variation in reproductive traits within the genus Todirostrum |journal=Journal of Natural History |date=12 March 2019 |volume=53 |issue=9–10 |pages=595–610 |doi=10.1080/00222933.2019.1599458 }}</ref> They thread plant material together into nests that hang from tree [[Branch|branches]].<ref name=":4" /> Nesting times vary by location, but nesting tends to occur between June and August and adults have been found in breeding conditions in February.<ref name=":5">{{Cite book |last1=Hilty |first1=S.L. |title=A guide to the birds of Colombia |last2=Brown |first2=W.L. |date=1986 |publisher=Princeton University Press |location=Princeton (NJ) |pages=481}}</ref> Clutches tend to be of a singular egg.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Solano-Ugalde |first1=Alejandro |last2=Greeney |first2=Harold |last3=Imaymana |first3=Fundación |date=2007 |title=Additional breeding records for selected avian species in northwest Ecuador |url=https://www.sao.org.co/publicaciones/boletinsao/AP2_XVII_1__2007.pdf |journal=Boletín de la Sociedad Antioqueña de Ornitología |volume=17 |pages=19–21 }}</ref> The egg is white with sparse yellow-brown spotting and rust ends.<ref name=":5" />

Occasionally, [[Piratic flycatcher|Piratic flycatchers (''Legatus leucophaius'')]] have usurped the black-headed tody-flycatchers' nests to use as their own. <ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last1=Sandoval |first1=Luis |last2=Barrantes |first2=Gilbert |date=2009 |title=Nest usurping occurrence of the Piratic Flycatcher (Legatus leucophaius) in southwestern Costa Rica |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/230562577 |journal=Ornitologia Neotropical |volume=20 |pages=401–407}}</ref> 

== Conservation ==
The IUCN most recently assessed it in 2021 and classified it as "least concern". Though its [[Population (biology)|population]] is declining, its wide [[Zoogeography|distribution]] and high estimated population numbers do not qualify it for the "[[Near-threatened species|near-threatened]]" status.<ref name=iucn/>
